aliases: Beef noodle salad with sesame dressing

english: Beef noodle salad with sesame dressing

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Chinese cold beef noodle salad; mapped to generic noodle dish class as closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (noodle-based dish with meat) used as closest defensible match; no specific code for Chinese cold beef noodle salad exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beef salad with noodles weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-serve lunch or dinner portion as sold by food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef noodle dish, composite estimate; supplemented by Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for dish-level validation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chinese cold beef noodle salad exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked beef (lean, boiled), cooked wheat noodles, and raw vegetables, combined in proportions consistent with the dish description. The input per_100g values (energy 128.89 kcal, protein 7.78 g, fat 4.89 g, carbohydrate 13.33 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 266.67 mg/100g reflects soy-sauce-based dressing contribution and is plausible. All values cross-checked against HPB Foodo noodle dish category ranges.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Wheat noodles (boiled) GI ~52 used as analogous estimate.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists for Chinese cold beef noodle salad as a composite dish. GI of 52 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~52, low category), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Cold preparation and vinegar-based dressing may further reduce effective GI by 5–10 units; conservative estimate of 52 retained. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (13.33 g/100g × 450 g = 60.0 g) / 100 = 31.2, rounded to 31.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.67 mg/100g driven by soy sauce dressing; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request low-sodium soy sauce, reduce dressing quantity, and increase the proportion of fresh vegetables such as cucumber and carrot to improve potassium offset; limit portion to half a bowl if sodium intake from other meals is already elevated.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wheat noodles contribute 13.33 g carbohydrate/100g (60 g per 450 g serving) with only 1.11 g dietary fibre/100g; GI of approximately 52 (low) is partially mitigated by vinegar in the dressing, but the high carbohydrate load per serving elevates glycaemic load to approximately 31.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to 150–200 g, increase the beef and vegetable ratio, consider substituting wheat noodles with konjac or soba noodles, and retain the vinegar-based dressing which modestly attenuates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.56 g/100g from beef and sesame oil contributes approximately 7 g per 450 g serving; sodium at 266.67 mg/100g (~1200 mg/serving) is a primary cardiovascular risk driver; trans fat 0.11 g/100g is low but present.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium, choose lean cuts of beef to reduce saturated fat, and omit chilli oil to minimise additional fat and sodium; the sesame oil and vinegar components provide modest cardioprotective benefit and should be retained.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; sodium at 266.67 mg/100g may exacerbate RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider replacing beef with tofu or oily fish to reduce arachidonic acid load, omit chilli oil if joint sensitivity is present, and retain sesame oil and vinegar which provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Salad with Noodles (香拌牛肉, Xiāng bàn niúròu) is a Chinese-style cold noodle dish popular in Singapore, featuring tender slices of boiled or blanched lean beef tossed with cooked wheat noodles and fresh vegetables such as julienned cucumber and carrot. The dish is dressed in a savoury and tangy sauce typically composed of soy sauce, sesame oil, rice vinegar, garlic, and chilli oil, giving it a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously umami-rich, slightly sour, mildly spicy, and aromatic. The noodles are served cold or at room temperature, making this a refreshing dish suited to warm climates. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 129 kcal, 7.78 g protein, 4.89 g fat, and 13.33 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 266.67 mg primarily from the soy sauce dressing. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low category) based on boiled wheat noodles, though the vinegar component may further attenuate postprandial glucose response. A standard 450 g bowl delivers approximately 580 kcal and 60 g carbohydrate, making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the beef content, and contains gluten (wheat noodles) and soy allergens. It is culturally associated with Chinese cuisine and is commonly available through food delivery platforms and hawker-adjacent restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chinese cold beef noodle salad exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy composite estimates for boiled beef, cooked wheat noodles, and fresh vegetables in proportions consistent with the dish description. The input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from boiled wheat noodles per the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Chinese cold beef noodle salad dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation is not reported in the primary approved sources consulted; estimated potassium ~145 mg/100g noted in input health suitability commentary but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo at the dish level as of 2024. trans_fat_g 0.11 g/100g is retained from input; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace trans fats from beef (naturally occurring vaccenic/conjugated linoleic acid) and is not set to -1. All core macros (protein_g 7.78, fat_g 4.89, carbohydrate_g 13.33) are non-zero and sourced from USDA composite adaptation; no zero-macro justification required. Halal status is marked false as beef sourcing and soy sauce brand cannot be confirmed as halal-certified from available data; consumers should verify with the specific vendor.
